title: People
text:
Associate Research Scientist
Department of Medical Oncology and Hematology
Yale School of Medicine · Yale Cancer Center
Guannan Gong leads research at the intersection of clinical informatics, artificial intelligence, and oncology. His work focuses on digital phenotyping from electronic health records, clinical trial patient matching, and scalable NLP methods for structuring trial eligibility criteria. He is the founder of CtrlTrial, which translates lab research into tools for real-time clinical trial recruitment.
Education

PhD, Computational Biology and Bioinformatics — Yale University (2021)
MS, Computer Science — Columbia University (2009)

Contact: guannan.gong@yale.edu · Yale profile · ORCID 0000-0002-4972-6705

title: People
text:
Collaborators
The Gong Lab works closely with investigators across Yale and Yale New Haven Health, including:

Wade Schulz, MD, PhD — Clinical informatics and biomedical data science
Harlan Krumholz, MD, SM — Outcomes research and health data science
Ian Krop, MD, PhD — Medical oncology and clinical trials
Maryam Lustberg, MD, MPH — Breast oncology and survivorship
Pamela Kunz, MD — Gastrointestinal oncology

We also collaborate with clinical trial teams, the Yale Cancer Center, and the Center for Outcomes Research and Evaluation (CORE).

title: People
text:
Trainees & students
We welcome motivated students and trainees interested in clinical NLP, machine learning, and oncology informatics. See Join the Lab for current opportunities.

title: The Gong Lab
text:
The Gong Lab at Yale School of Medicine develops artificial intelligence and clinical informatics methods to improve cancer clinical trial matching, real-world evidence generation, oncology trial design, and equitable access to precision cancer care.
Led by Guannan Gong, PhD, our work integrates electronic health records, clinical natural language processing, large language models, real-world data, and clinical trial evidence synthesis. We build tools that help research teams identify eligible patients faster, structure complex trial eligibility criteria at scale, and translate biomedical data into actionable insights for clinicians and trial sponsors.



Clinical trial patient matching — Real-time, OMOP-based systems that prescreen cancer patients against open trials using structured and unstructured EHR data
Eligibility criteria intelligence — NLP and LLM pipelines to structure, cluster, and visualize trial eligibility criteria across disease domains
Real-world data & EHR science — Computational phenotyping, data integrity assessment, and predictive modeling for clinical research
Equitable trial access — Methods designed to reach underserved and underrepresented patient populations





Yale School of Medicine — Medical Oncology and Hematology
Yale Cancer Center
Yale Computational Biology and Biomedical Informatics





2026 — Clinical Trial Patient Matching system validated across 29 oncology trials (JCO Clinical Cancer Informatics)
2025 — Blavatnik Accelerator Award for translational biotech innovation
2024 — Yale New Haven Health Innovation Award for clinical trial patient matching

Learn about our research →

title: Contact
text:
Collaboration inquiries
For research collaborations, data partnerships, or clinical trial informatics projects, please email Dr. Gong directly. For trainee and student opportunities, see Join the Lab.

title: Research
text: The Gong Lab develops computational methods that connect patients to the right clinical trials, improve the quality of real-world evidence, and help design more efficient oncology studies. Our research spans method development, health system implementation, and translational evaluation in cancer care.

title: Research
text:
Clinical trial patient matching
Only 5–7% of adult cancer patients enroll in clinical trials, despite rapid growth in available studies. Manual chart review for eligibility screening is a major bottleneck.
We develop Clinical Trial Patient Matching (CTPM) systems that combine rule-based logic and clinical NLP to prescreen patients against trial inclusion and exclusion criteria. Our pipeline uses electronic health record data standardized to the Observational Medical Outcomes Partnership (OMOP) common data model, enabling structured and unstructured data to be analyzed together across health systems.
In validation work published in JCO Clinical Cancer Informatics, our system was evaluated on metastatic colorectal cancer trials and subsequently deployed across 29 oncology trials spanning multiple cancer types and phases. The approach lets research teams focus on high-likelihood candidates rather than exhaustive manual review.

title: Research
text:
Eligibility criteria intelligence
Trial eligibility criteria are complex, heterogeneous, and difficult to compare across protocols. We build scalable NLP frameworks that:

Extract and normalize eligibility text from clinical trial registries
Cluster semantically similar criteria using embedding-based methods
Summarize criteria patterns with large language models
Visualize domain-level trends through interactive web interfaces

This work supports automated patient matching and evidence-based trial design by revealing recurring inclusion and exclusion patterns across oncology domains (e.g., breast, lung, and GI malignancies).

title: Research
text:
Real-world data & EHR science
High-quality clinical research depends on trustworthy EHR data. We study:

Computational phenotyping from structured and unstructured clinical data
Real-time EHR data integrity for research use
Digital phenotyping for adverse event detection and patient identification
Predictive modeling with NLP and machine learning for precision medicine

Our EHR science work is conducted in close collaboration with Yale New Haven Health and the Schulz Lab.

title: Research
text:
Equitable access to clinical trials
We design matching systems with diversity and equity as explicit goals—recognizing underserved groups, supporting multilingual populations, and reducing barriers that limit trial access. CtrlTrial, our translational platform, embeds these principles into real-time notification workflows for clinicians and trial teams.

title: Research
text:
Methods & technologies







Area
Methods




Data standards
OMOP CDM, FHIR integration


NLP / AI
Clinical NLP, large language models, hybrid rules + ML pipelines


Data sources
EHR (Epic), pathology, laboratory, CTMS, ClinicalTrials.gov


Domains
Oncology clinical trials, real-world evidence, outcomes research

title: Research
text:
Funding & support
Our research is supported by the Yale Cancer Center Translational-Targeted Area of Research Excellence (T-TARE), Yale New Haven Health Innovation Awards, the Yale Cancer Center Catchment Area Research Award, and the Blavatnik Fund for Innovation at Yale.

title: Join the Lab
text: The Gong Lab welcomes trainees and collaborators who are passionate about using AI and informatics to improve cancer care and clinical trial access.

title: Join the Lab
text:
Who we’re looking for
We are interested in working with students and researchers who have backgrounds in:

Computer science, bioinformatics, or computational biology
Clinical informatics and health data science
Natural language processing and machine learning
Biostatistics and epidemiology
Medical oncology or related clinical fields

Prior experience with electronic health records, OMOP, or clinical NLP is helpful but not required for all projects.

title: Join the Lab
text:
Opportunities
Yale graduate students & postdocs
We collaborate with programs in Computational Biology and Biomedical Informatics (CBB), Biomedical Informatics & Data Science, and related Yale departments. Contact Dr. Gong to discuss rotation projects, thesis work, or postdoctoral opportunities.
Yale medical and undergraduate students
We regularly mentor students on clinical informatics projects, including eligibility criteria NLP, trial matching evaluation, and EHR data analysis. Prior students have contributed to published protocols and software tools.
Visiting scholars & collaborators
We welcome collaborations with investigators at other institutions working on clinical trial informatics, real-world evidence, or oncology AI.

title: Join the Lab
text:
How to apply
Send an email to guannan.gong@yale.edu with:

A brief description of your research interests
Your CV or resume
Relevant code samples or publications (if available)

We review inquiries on a rolling basis and will follow up if there is a potential fit.

title: Projects
text:
Our flagship research platform for semiautomated prescreening of cancer patients against open clinical trials.
What it does

Ingests structured and unstructured EHR data via the OMOP common data model
Applies hybrid rules-based and NLP logic to evaluate inclusion/exclusion criteria
Delivers real-time feasibility assessments and patient-trial match lists
Scales across trials, cancer types, and health system deployments

Impact
Validated on metastatic colorectal cancer trials and extended to 29 oncology studies. Supported by Yale Cancer Center T-TARE and YNHH Innovation Awards.
Read the 2026 publication →

title: Projects
text:
CtrlTrial
CtrlTrial is a translational platform founded by Dr. Gong to bring AI-driven clinical trial matching from Yale research into health system practice.
Capabilities

Real-time patient prescreening from Epic and integrated clinical systems
Clinician and trial team notifications when patients become eligible
Customizable search parameters for trial-specific criteria
Analytics on enrollment trends and diversity metrics
API integration with EHR, pathology, laboratory, and CTMS systems

CtrlTrial was developed through Yale Tsai CITY programs and validated in pilot deployments with Yale New Haven Health. It won the 2024 Yale New Haven Health Innovation Award.
Visit CtrlTrial →

title: Projects
text:
Eligibility Criteria Visualizer
An NLP framework for structuring and exploring clinical trial eligibility criteria at scale.
Approach

Unsupervised clustering of semantically similar criteria from trial protocols
LLM-based summarization of criteria clusters
Interactive visualization of patterns by disease domain and over time

The prototype analyzed 53,000+ oncology trials from ClinicalTrials.gov, including breast, lung, and GI oncology domains. Open-source tools are available via the CriteriaVisualizer repository.
Funded by the Yale New Haven Health Innovation Award (2024–2026).
Read the study protocol →

title: Projects
text:
Real-time EHR data integrity
A collaborative project with the Schulz Lab assessing whether real-time EHR extracts meet the quality standards required for clinical research—benchmarking latency, completeness, and accuracy against research-grade datasets.
Publication →

title: Projects
text:
Research informatics infrastructure
We contribute to Yale-wide informatics initiatives including:

Patient recruitment workflows connecting primary care providers through the EMR and patient portal
Real-time specimen identification for collaborative biobanking
Computational phenotyping pipelines for precision medicine

These projects build on Dr. Gong’s experience at the Yale/YNHH Center for Outcomes Research and Evaluation (CORE).

title: Publications
text: Selected peer-reviewed publications from the Gong Lab. A full list is available on Dr. Gong’s Yale profile.

title: Publications
text:
2026
Clinical Trial Patient Matching: A Real-Time, Common Data Model and Artificial Intelligence–Driven System for Semiautomated Patient Prescreening in Cancer Clinical Trials
Gong G, Liu J, Pandya S, Taborda C, Wiesendanger N, Price N, Byron W, Coppi A, Young P, Wiess C, Dunning H, Barganier C, Brodeur R, Fischbach N, LoRusso P, Pusztai L, Kim S, Rozenblit M, Cecchini M, Mongiu A, Mendez L, Kaftan E, Torre C, Krumholz H, Krop I, Schulz W, Lustberg M, Kunz P.
JCO Clinical Cancer Informatics 2026; 10:e2500262.
PubMed · DOI: 10.1200/CCI-25-00262
Developed and validated a hybrid rules- and NLP-based CTPM pipeline using OMOP-standardized EHR data, demonstrating improved recruitment efficiency across 29 oncology trials.

Assessment of the integrity of real-time electronic health record data used in clinical research
Liu J, Pandya S, Coppi A, Young H, Krumholz H, Schulz W, Gong G.
PLOS ONE 2026; 21:e0340287.
PubMed · DOI: 10.1371/journal.pone.0340287
Benchmarked the accuracy and reliability of real-time EHR data for secondary use in clinical research.

A Natural Language Processing Framework for Structuring and Visualizing Clinical Trial Eligibility Criteria at Scale: Protocol for a Quantitative Study
Xie J, Parikh J, Liu J, Pandya S, Gong G.
JMIR Research Protocols 2026.
Full text
Protocol describing an LLM-enabled pipeline for clustering, summarizing, and visualizing eligibility criteria from ClinicalTrials.gov oncology trials.
